## Summary

Multi-target eval runs now fan out into isolated target/variant result bundles under the invocation timestamp, so two targets with the same `test_id` no longer point at and overwrite the same sidecar files. Within each bundle, row sidecar directories use deterministic compact row IDs based on eval source, suite, test id, target, and variant, while `index.jsonl` remains the source of truth for identity and artifact paths.

Dashboard and result readers now discover nested bundle manifests under timestamp folders while preserving legacy root-level `index.jsonl` bundles. They may walk storage folders to find manifests, but target and variant semantics continue to come from loaded row/run metadata, not path parsing.

Fixes https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv/issues/1557.

## Validation

    ### worker-ownership-convention
    assignee is the exclusive active worker lock — do not edit a Bead assigned to another active worker unless deliberately taking over and leaving a comment. Set metadata.machine (host/VM), metadata.worker (Workmux handle/session/agent), metadata.branch (active branch), and metadata.worktree (worktree path or handle) when claiming a
    Bead. Clear assignee when finished.


    # 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

    **CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

    ```
    [ ] 1. bd close <id1> <id2> ...   (close completed issues)
    [ ] 2. run quality gates        (tests, linters, builds when relevant)
    [ ] 3. git status               (check what changed)
    [ ] 4. follow active profile    (conservative: report handoff; team-maintainer: commit/sync/push if enabled)
    ```

    **Policy:** Conservative is the default. Commit, sync, or push only when the active user, orchestrator, or repository profile grants that authority.

    ## Core Rules
    - **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
    - **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
    - **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
    - **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
    - Persistence you don't need beats lost context
    - Default: do not commit, push, or run dolt remote sync without explicit authority. Team-maintainer behavior is opt-in and still subordinate to user/orchestrator instructions.
    - Git workflow: conservative by default; commit/push only with explicit user/orchestrator or team-maintainer authority
    - Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

    ## Essential Commands

    ### Finding Work
    - `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
    - `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
    - `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
    - `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

    ### Creating & Updating
    - `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
      - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
    - `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
    - `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
    - `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
    - `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
    - `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
    - `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
    - **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
    - **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

    ### Dependencies & Blocking
    - `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
    - `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
    - `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this issue

    ### Sync & Collaboration
    - `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
    - `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads from Dolt remote
    - `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

    ### Project Health
    - `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked counts)
    - `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems, missing hooks)
    - `bd doctor --check=conventions` - Check for convention drift (lint, stale, orphans)

    ### Quality Tools
    - `bd create --validate` - Check description has required sections
    - `bd create --acceptance="criteria"` - Set acceptance criteria (checked by --validate)
    - `bd create --design="decisions"` - Record design decisions
    - `bd create --notes="context"` - Add supplementary notes
    - `bd config set validation.on-create warn` - Auto-validate on every create
    - `bd lint` - Check existing issues for missing sections

    ### Lifecycle & Hygiene
    - `bd defer <id> --until="date"` - Defer work to a future date
    - `bd supersede <id> --with=<new-id>` - Mark issue as superseded
    - `bd close <id> --suggest-next` - Show newly unblocked issues after closing
    - `bd stale` - Find issues with no recent activity
    - `bd orphans` - Find issues with broken dependencies
    - `bd preflight` - Pre-PR checks (lint, stale, orphans)
    - `bd human <id>` - Flag for human decision (list/respond/dismiss)

    ### Structured Workflows
    - `bd formula list` - See available workflow templates
    - `bd mol pour <name>` - Start structured workflow from formula

    ## Common Workflows

    **Starting work:**
    ```bash
    bd ready           # Find available work
    bd show <id>       # Review issue details
    bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
    ```

    **Completing work:**
    ```bash
    bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
    git status                  # Check changed files
    # Conservative/minimal/default: report status and proposed commands; wait for approval
    # Team-maintainer opt-in only, unless current instructions forbid it:
    # git add . && git commit -m "..."
    # bd dolt push
    # git push
    ```

    **Creating dependent work:**
    ```bash
    # Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
    bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
    bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
    bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
    ```

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

# AGENTS.md - AI Research Wiki

This is the root instruction file for all AI coding/research agents working in this wiki-style knowledge base.

## Project Overview
- **Purpose**: Curated, compounding knowledge base for AI research, papers, tools, and experiments.
- **Style**: Clear, concise, actionable Markdown with heavy use of links, tables, and code blocks.
- **Philosophy**: Progressive disclosure + hierarchical instructions (root + nested).

## Recommended Folder Structure

```
ai-research-wiki/
├── AGENTS.md                          # ← Root index + global rules (this file)
├── .agents/                           # Shared modular instructions
│   ├── unit-tests.md
│   ├── security.md
│   ├── pr-checklist.md
│   ├── citation-style.md
│   └── paper-summary-template.md
├── sources/                           # Raw inputs (papers, transcripts, etc.)
│   ├── papers/
│   ├── podcasts/
│   └── articles/
├── wiki/                              # Compiled / curated knowledge
│   ├── concepts/

│   └── techniques/
├── experiments/                       # Active research projects
│   ├── experiment-001/
│   └── ...
├── frontend/                          # (if applicable)
│   └── AGENTS.md
├── backend/                           # (if applicable)
│   └── AGENTS.md
└── README.md
```

## Data Sources (Priority Order)

1. **Primary** — Peer-reviewed papers (arXiv, NeurIPS, ICML, etc.)
2. **High-quality** — Official documentation, GitHub repos, and technical blogs from reputable sources
3. **Supporting** — Podcasts, video transcripts (e.g., Lex Fridman, Karpathy), Twitter/X threads
4. **Experimental** — Your own notes, benchmarks, and reproduction attempts

## Agent Routing Rules (Root as Index)

- For **paper summaries or literature review** → Read `@wiki/paper-summary-template.md`
- For **new experiment setup** → Read `@experiments/AGENTS.md` (or nearest)
- For **citation & sourcing** → Read `@.agents/citation-style.md`
- For **code implementation** → Read nearest subfolder `AGENTS.md`
- For **PRs & contributions** → Read `@.agents/pr-checklist.md`

**Always combine root rules with the most specific nested `AGENTS.md`.**

+
+Then publish a concise, well-sourced writeup into ai-research-wiki and push a branch/PR.
+

+- Branch: research/wfai-eval-architecture-agentv
+- Worktree handle: wfai-eval-architecture-research

+- You are a research/doc worker. Do not implement AgentV feature code.
+- You may read /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv for AgentV architecture and docs.
+- Do not modify /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv.
+- Do not run AgentV builds/tests/evals unless you need a targeted command to verify a claim; if you do, record why.
+- Keep generated evidence or temporary files out of public repos.
+- Respect dirty checkouts; do not revert unrelated changes.
+
+Repo instructions:
+- Read ai-research-wiki/AGENTS.md.
+- AGENTS.md references .agents/citation-style.md and .agents/pr-checklist.md, but they are missing in the current checkout. Note that gap if relevant and follow the root style: clear, concise Markdown with links, tables, and actionable synthesis.
+- Use existing wiki conventions. Search existing files under concepts/, entities/, comparisons/, and raw/articles/.
+- Likely output location: comparisons/ or concepts/. Pick the closest existing location and explain the choice in your handoff.
+
+Research sources:
+1. Primary WFAI source:
+   - Use the GitHub URL above. Fetch the exact J55 branch file.
+   - Cite the file URL and relevant section headings/claims.
+2. AgentV source:
+   - /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv/AGENTS.md
+   - /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv/STRATEGY.md
+   - /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv/ROADMAP.md

+   - Relevant AgentV docs under apps/web/src/content/docs/

+3. Existing wiki sources:
+   - comparisons/eval-frameworks-benchmark.md
+   - concepts/evaluation-frameworks-landscape.md
+   - concepts/production-llm-evaluation-observability-loop.md

+   - raw/articles/vercel-agent-eval.md
+   - raw/articles/margin-evals.md
+
+Writeup requirements:
+- State what WFAI appears to require from an eval system.
+- Map each WFAI use case/capability to AgentV status:
+  - Supported now
+  - Partially supported / needs composition
+  - Not supported / outside boundary
+- Assess alignment with AgentV architecture:
+  - repo-native evals
+  - zero-infra local-to-CI
+  - portable run artifacts
+  - dashboard as zero-infra inspection
+  - adapter boundaries instead of absorbing external concepts
+  - project vs benchmark terminology
+  - target/provider/grader/result artifact boundaries
+- Recommend any AgentV architecture changes, if warranted. Be conservative: prefer composition patterns/docs/plugins unless a core primitive is clearly missing.
+- Call out if WFAI suggests architecture that conflicts with AgentV product boundaries.
+- Include an “Implications for AgentV” section with concrete follow-up Bead suggestions, but do not create extra Beads unless a blocker requires it.
+
+Validation / deliverable:
+- Commit the writeup on the branch.
+- Push the branch to origin.
+- Open a draft PR in tsoyang-org/ai-research-wiki.
+- Update Bead av-zd2 with:
+  - wiki worktree path
+  - branch
+  - commit SHA
+  - PR URL
+  - source file path(s)
+  - any recommended AgentV follow-up issues

+Do not claim unsupported details. If the WFAI file cannot be fetched, record the exact error and use no speculation.

    ## Core Rules
    - **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd
    create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
    - **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or
    markdown files for task tracking
    - **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code,
    mark in_progress when starting
    - **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for
    persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use
    MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search
    with `bd memories <keyword>`.
    - Persistence you don't need beats lost context
    - Profile model: conservative/minimal report handoff;
    team-maintainer may commit only when explicitly enabled
    - Git workflow: conservative by default on ephemeral
    branches
    - Session management: check `bd ready` for available
    work

    ## Essential Commands

    ### Finding Work
    - `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
    - `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
    - `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
    - `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with
    dependencies

    ### Creating & Updating
    - `bd create --title="Summary of this issue"
    --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to
    be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New
    issue
      - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium,
      4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
    - `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
    - `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to
    someone
    - `bd update <id>
    --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields
    inline
    - `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
    - `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at
    once (more efficient)
    - `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with
    reason
    - **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics,
    use parallel subagents for efficiency
    - **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR
    (vim/nano) which blocks agents

    ### Dependencies & Blocking
    - `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency
    (issue depends on depends-on)
    - `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
    - `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this
    issue

    ### Sync & Collaboration
    - `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads updates from Dolt remote
    - `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
    - `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

    ### Project Health
    - `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked
    counts)
    - `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems,
    missing hooks)
    - `bd doctor --check=conventions` - Check for
    convention drift (lint, stale, orphans)

    ### Quality Tools
    - `bd create --validate` - Check description has
    required sections
    - `bd create --acceptance="criteria"` - Set acceptance
    criteria (checked by --validate)
    - `bd create --design="decisions"` - Record design
    decisions
    - `bd create --notes="context"` - Add supplementary
    notes
    - `bd config set validation.on-create warn` - Auto-
    validate on every create
    - `bd lint` - Check existing issues for missing
    sections

    ### Lifecycle & Hygiene
    - `bd defer <id> --until="date"` - Defer work to a
    future date
    - `bd supersede <id> --with=<new-id>` - Mark issue as
    superseded
    - `bd close <id> --suggest-next` - Show newly unblocked
    issues after closing
    - `bd stale` - Find issues with no recent activity
    - `bd orphans` - Find issues with broken dependencies
    - `bd preflight` - Pre-PR checks (lint, stale, orphans)
    - `bd human <id>` - Flag for human decision (list/
    respond/dismiss)

    ### Structured Workflows
    - `bd formula list` - See available workflow templates
    - `bd mol pour <name>` - Start structured workflow from
    formula

    ## Common Workflows

    **Starting work:**
    ```bash
    bd ready           # Find available work
    bd show <id>       # Review issue details
    bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
    ```

    **Completing work:**
    ```bash
    bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed
    issues at once
    bd dolt pull                # Pull latest beads from
    main
    git status                  # Report changed files and
    proposed commit; wait for authority
    # Merge to main locally only when the active
    instructions grant that authority
    ```

    **Creating dependent work:**
    ```bash
    # Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for
    many items)
    bd create --title="Implement feature X"
    --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to
    be done" --type=feature
    bd create --title="Write tests for X"
    --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to
    be done" --type=task
    bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on
    Feature (Feature blocks tests)
    ```

## Summary

Multi-target eval runs now fan out into isolated target/variant result bundles under the invocation timestamp, so two targets with the same `test_id` no longer point at and overwrite the same sidecar files. Within each bundle, row sidecar directories use deterministic compact row IDs based on eval source, suite, test id, target, and variant, while `index.jsonl` remains the source of truth for identity and artifact paths.

Dashboard and result readers now discover nested bundle manifests under timestamp folders while preserving legacy root-level `index.jsonl` bundles. They may walk storage folders to find manifests, but target and variant semantics continue to come from loaded row/run metadata, not path parsing.

Fixes https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv/issues/1557.

✓ av-9vi [BUG] · Fix target-aware result artifact layout and document storage ADR   [● P1 · CLOSED]
Owner: Christopher Tso · Assignee: Christopher Tso · Type: bug
Created: 2026-06-28 · Started: 2026-06-28 · Updated: 2026-06-29
Close reason: Implementation PR #1558 refreshed on current origin/main, live proxy provider/grader dogfood passed, local gates and GitHub Actions are green; merge intentionally left to reviewer/coordinator.
External: https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv/issues/1557

DESCRIPTION
Why this exists:
Dogfood research confirmed AgentV multi-target runs currently preserve target in index metadata but write different targets for the same test_id to the same per-case sidecar directory. In the temp CLI run, mock-alpha and mock-beta both pointed at case-one/run-1/*. The second target overwrote outputs/answer.md, grading.json, metrics.json, timing.json, and case summary for the first target.

What needs to be done:
Treat this as a minimal bug fix, not a broad result-format redesign. Make the artifact subdirectory allocator target/variant-aware for sidecars when the same case can appear under multiple targets or variants, keep existing experiment/timestamp/run-N semantics, preserve legacy readers, and update the storage ADR with the researched alternatives. Source evidence is linked through av-770 (Vercel vs AgentV dogfood), av-e49 (Margin Evals layout research), and av-74h (upstream Vercel multi-model/variant smoke).

Likely code surface:
- packages/core/src/evaluation/run-artifacts.ts, especially buildArtifactSubdir/result sidecar path generation.
- Result index/summary path fields that reference grading_path, metrics_path, outputs, transcripts, timing, and case summaries.
- Dashboard/result readers that resolve sidecar paths, with backward compatibility for old case/run-N paths.

DESIGN
Chosen direction:
AgentV should be a superset of Vercel-style experiment naming, not locked to it. Keep .agentv/results/<experiment>/ as the comparison campaign namespace and keep experiment: in eval YAML. Do not rename experiment to run_group. Within an experiment, keep timestamp as the invocation/batch folder, then use structured target and optional variant folders as isolated run bundles.

Preferred writer layout:
.agentv/results/<experiment>/<timestamp>/<target>/<variant?>/
  index.jsonl
  summary.json
  <row_id>/run-1/
  <row_id>/run-2/

A multi-target CLI command may still be ergonomic, but it should fan out into target/variant run bundles under the timestamp rather than requiring all targets to share one index.jsonl. This supports running one comparable now, resuming later to fill missing target/variant cells, and changing Dashboard comparison views without rewriting or pushing changes to the results repo. Path gives coarse isolation; index.jsonl gives exact meaning.

row_id should be stable, filesystem-safe, and compact, e.g. <safe_test_id>--<short_hash>. The hash input must include collision-prone row fields available inside that target/variant bundle, at minimum eval_path/source eval identity, suite label, and test_id; include target and variant too if present in row metadata for compatibility. The visible test_id prefix is for human scanning only.

Rationale:
This is simpler and more fluid than either putting all targets in one timestamp bundle or encoding all variants into experiment names. Vercel next-evals-oss can be represented by experiment names such as <experiment>--agents-md, while AgentV also supports structured target/variant dimensions. Margin Evals validates manifest-first storage. AgentV becomes the superset: structured physical isolation plus manifest-driven comparison.

Alternatives considered:
1. Vercel agent-eval upstream: CLI model arrays become separate experiment paths, <config>/<model>/<timestamp>/<case>/run-N. Good for model-as-experiment publication but fragments one comparison campaign unless export logic pairs them.
2. Vercel next-evals-oss downstream: one experiment file per model and --agents-md variant. Good for public baseline publication and memoization, but it is downstream policy. AgentV should allow it by experiment naming, not require it.
3. Margin Evals: one output run directory with manifests and no experiment folder. Good manifest-first reference, but AgentV still benefits from an experiment campaign namespace and local Dashboard grouping.
4. AgentV current behavior: target in metadata only and shared sidecar paths. Confirmed broken because sidecar files collide.

NOTES
Updated conclusion: AgentV should be the superset. Keep experiment as campaign namespace, timestamp as invocation/batch, and target/variant as structured isolated run-bundle folders: .agentv/results/<experiment>/<timestamp>/<target>/<variant?>/. Path provides storage isolation and manual browsing only; index.jsonl/run metadata provides semantics. Dashboard/readers must not derive target or variant by parsing folder names. Discovery may use folders to find index.jsonl files, but loaded row/run metadata is authoritative for target, variant, eval_path, suite, test_id, and sidecar paths.

Dashboard completeness is in scope: nested index discovery, legacy root index compatibility, run detail/drilldown, compare across target/variant bundles under one experiment, and browser UAT. Dogfood plan: focused local two-target eval, same-test-id/suite collision test, Dashboard browser UAT, remote results push/load from agentv/results/v1 after clearing local materialized cache, and live provider/grader eval before ready-for-review unless blocked.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
A multi-target CLI run no longer overwrites per-target artifacts.
New writes isolate target and optional variant under .agentv/results/<experiment>/<timestamp>/<target>/<variant?>/.
Each target/variant run bundle has its own index.jsonl, summary.json, and deterministic row sidecar directories.
Row IDs are deterministic, preferably <safe_test_id>--<short_hash>, and hash eval_path/source eval identity, suite label, test_id, and relevant row identity dimensions.
Existing runs remain readable through their index.jsonl path fields. Readers that infer <case>/run-N instead of following index.jsonl are updated.
Dashboard/result readers discover nested index.jsonl bundles under timestamp folders while preserving root-level legacy bundle discovery.
Dashboard uses row/run metadata for target and variant semantics; it must not parse target/variant from folder names.
Dashboard can list, open, and compare target/variant run bundles under the same experiment without requiring results repo rewrites.
Tests cover multi-target fanout/isolation, same test_id across two suites, duplicate suite labels from different eval paths, variant when existing plumbing exposes variant, and metadata-not-path target/variant behavior.
Browser UAT verifies a project with nested target/variant bundles loads in Dashboard and compare/drilldown works.
Remote-sync dogfood verifies nested bundles pushed to agentv/results/v1 can be loaded after clearing local materialized results/cache.
Live eval dogfood with a real provider/grader is run before ready-for-review, or any blocker is explicitly recorded with commands/evidence.
ADR/docs clarify that AgentV supports Vercel-style experiment naming and structured target/variant dimensions as a superset.

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv/.workmux/prompts/examples-oracle-fixtures.md
+You are the implementation worker for AgentV Bead av-o6n.
+
+Task:
+Every runnable example eval in AgentV should have a deterministic oracle/reference fixture so examples can be run with an oracle target override and no live LLM calls. This should let maintainers cheaply prove that example evals still parse, execute, write artifacts, and remain compatible with the current eval contract.
+
+Tracker:
+- Bead: av-o6n
+- Update av-o6n with branch, worktree, commits, PR URL, verification commands, and any exclusions.
+
+Branch/worktree:
+- Branch: feat/example-oracle-fixtures
+- Worktree: /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv__worktrees/example-oracle-fixtures
+
+Repository rules:
+- Read AGENTS.md plus relevant .agents guides before product-boundary decisions.
+- Start with git fetch origin and git status --short --branch.
+- Use bun for package/script operations.
+- Do not modify the main checkout.
+- Main checkout has pre-existing dirty showcase files; do not touch or revert those from the main checkout.
+- Keep all implementation in this worktree and stage explicit paths only.
+- Do not push directly to main.
+- Open/update a PR when ready.
+
+Intent:
+- Add an oracle/reference mechanism for examples, not a broad eval architecture rewrite.
+- Prefer existing AgentV primitives. Search for existing oracle/reference/mock/replay/static target support before inventing a new provider.
+- If a tiny helper target/provider/script is needed, keep it narrow and documented.
+- Live provider dogfood remains separate; this work is about cheap deterministic example execution.
+
+Required first step:
+1. Inventory every eval file under examples/ (for example *.eval.yaml and any TS/JSON eval definitions).
+2. Classify each as:
+   - Runnable with oracle fixture
+   - Needs fixture added
+   - Excluded, with exact reason (external service, workspace setup, intentionally live-only, etc.)
+3. Record the inventory in the Bead and in a repo-appropriate artifact if useful (docs, test fixture, or generated manifest checked into repo only if it is meant to be maintained).
+
+Implementation expectations:
+- Add oracle/reference files next to example evals or in a clear shared location.
+- Provide a standard override workflow so examples can run against the oracle target without editing the eval YAML by hand.
+- The override should be easy to call in CI/local scripts.
+- Ensure wire-format remains snake_case and TypeScript internals camelCase.
+- Preserve existing example semantics; do not remove live provider paths.
+- Avoid hard-coding current temp paths or machine-local configuration.
+
+Verification expectations:
+- Add or update tests/scripts so at least a representative set, and ideally every runnable example eval, can run with the oracle target and no LLM provider keys.
+- Use `bun apps/cli/src/cli.ts ...` for CLI functional testing. If core changes are made, run `bun run build` first.
+- Run focused tests for changed code and the oracle example sweep.
+- Run broader checks if core/CLI shared behavior changes.
+- If any examples are excluded, make exclusions explicit in docs/output and explain whether follow-up Beads are needed.
+
+Acceptance:
+- Every runnable example eval has an oracle/reference fixture or equivalent deterministic source.
+- There is one clear command/workflow to run examples with the oracle target override and no live LLM calls.
+- The command proves examples can run in the current shape without provider credentials.
+- PR includes docs or instructions for the workflow.
+- Bead av-o6n records inventory, exclusions, commands run, branch, commit, and PR.
+
+Before handoff:
+- Rebase/update onto current origin/main.
+- Push the branch.
+- Open a draft PR or ready PR depending on verification completeness.
+- Leave the worktree clean except expected ignored outputs.
*** End Patch

  hook context: [bd prime] If this output is truncated by your host, read the full persisted hook output before continuing; it may contain project memories and session rules not visible in the preview.

    # Beads Workflow Context

    > **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
    > Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code and Codex when a beads workspace is resolved


    ## Persistent Memories (1)

    Stored via `bd remember`. Update in place with `bd remember --key <key> "new content"`. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`. Remove with `bd forget <key>`.

    ### worker-ownership-convention
    assignee is the exclusive active worker lock — do not edit a Bead assigned to another active worker unless deliberately taking over and leaving a comment. Set metadata.machine (host/VM), metadata.worker (Workmux handle/session/agent), metadata.branch (active branch), and metadata.worktree (worktree path or handle) when claiming a
    Bead. Clear assignee when finished.


    # 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

    **CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

    ```
    [ ] 1. bd close <id1> <id2> ...   (close completed issues)
    [ ] 2. run quality gates        (tests, linters, builds when relevant)
    [ ] 3. git status               (check what changed)
    [ ] 4. report handoff           (changed files, validation, proposed commit if authorized)
    ```

    **Note:** This is an ephemeral branch (no upstream). Do not push it unless the user or orchestrator explicitly says to.

    ## Core Rules
    - **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
    - **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
    - **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
    - **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
    - Persistence you don't need beats lost context
    - Profile model: conservative/minimal report handoff; team-maintainer may commit only when explicitly enabled
    - Git workflow: conservative by default on ephemeral branches
    - Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

    ## Essential Commands

    ### Finding Work
    - `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
    - `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
    - `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
    - `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

    ### Creating & Updating
    - `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
      - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
    - `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
    - `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
    - `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
    - `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
    - `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
    - `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
    - **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
    - **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

    ### Dependencies & Blocking
    - `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
    - `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
    - `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this issue

    ### Sync & Collaboration
    - `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads updates from Dolt remote
    - `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
    - `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

    ### Project Health
    - `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked counts)
    - `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems, missing hooks)
    - `bd doctor --check=conventions` - Check for convention drift (lint, stale, orphans)

    ### Quality Tools
    - `bd create --validate` - Check description has required sections
    - `bd create --acceptance="criteria"` - Set acceptance criteria (checked by --validate)
    - `bd create --design="decisions"` - Record design decisions
    - `bd create --notes="context"` - Add supplementary notes
    - `bd config set validation.on-create warn` - Auto-validate on every create
    - `bd lint` - Check existing issues for missing sections

    ### Lifecycle & Hygiene
    - `bd defer <id> --until="date"` - Defer work to a future date
    - `bd supersede <id> --with=<new-id>` - Mark issue as superseded
    - `bd close <id> --suggest-next` - Show newly unblocked issues after closing
    - `bd stale` - Find issues with no recent activity
    - `bd orphans` - Find issues with broken dependencies
    - `bd preflight` - Pre-PR checks (lint, stale, orphans)
    - `bd human <id>` - Flag for human decision (list/respond/dismiss)

    ### Structured Workflows
    - `bd formula list` - See available workflow templates
    - `bd mol pour <name>` - Start structured workflow from formula

    ## Common Workflows

    **Starting work:**
    ```bash
    bd ready           # Find available work
    bd show <id>       # Review issue details
    bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
    ```

    **Completing work:**
    ```bash
    bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
    bd dolt pull                # Pull latest beads from main
    git status                  # Report changed files and proposed commit; wait for authority
    # Merge to main locally only when the active instructions grant that authority
    ```

    **Creating dependent work:**
    ```bash
    # Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
    bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
    bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
    bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
    ```

docs/solutions/best-practices/name-portable-config-endpoints-by-user-intent.md:26:For a checked-in AgentV project config, requiring a local Git remote alias would make the file depend on per-machine setup. docs/solutions/best-practices/name-portable-config-endpoints-by-user-intent.md:32:For AgentV results repositories, use `results.repo.remote` for the Git endpoint URL:

docs/solutions/best-practices/name-portable-config-endpoints-by-user-intent.md:49:Keep established neighboring contracts intact unless they are part of the same user-facing block. docs/solutions/best-practices/name-portable-config-endpoints-by-user-intent.md:53:Portable config is commonly checked into a repository, copied into Docker development environments, or generated by setup scripts. A field that accepts a machine-local alias forces every machine and container to create the same Git remote name before the config works.

---
title: "Name portable config endpoints by user intent"
module: configuration contracts
date: 2026-06-22
problem_type: best_practice
component: tooling
severity: medium
tags:
  - configuration
  - portability
  - git-remotes
  - results
  - dashboard
applies_when:
  - Designing persisted YAML or JSON config that will be shared across machines
  - Adding Git-backed storage, sync, or artifact configuration
  - Choosing between exposing an endpoint URL and exposing a local runtime alias
---

# Name portable config endpoints by user intent

## Context

While adding Git-backed dashboard result remotes, the initial nested results repository config used `results.repo.url` for the Git endpoint URL and left `remote` available for the local Git remote-name implementation detail. That split was technically precise but did not match how users talk about portable Git sync configuration: in related tools such as Beads, `remote` names the shared endpoint URL, not a machine-local alias.

For a checked-in AgentV project config, requiring a local Git remote alias would make the file depend on per-machine setup. The portable contract only needs the endpoint URL, a local path, and the branch/ref that stores artifacts.

## Guidance

For persisted project and eval configuration, prefer field names that describe the user-facing contract and remain portable across machines. Keep local runtime mechanics out of normal config.

For AgentV results repositories, use `results.repo.remote` for the Git endpoint URL:

```yaml
projects:
  - id: academy-evals
    repo:
      url: https://github.com/WiseTechAcademy/Evals.git
      branch: main
    results:
      repo:
        remote: https://github.com/WiseTechAcademy/EvalResults.git
        path: .agentv/remotes/academy-results
        branch: agentv/results/v1
```

Internally, the runtime can still create or use a Git remote name such as `origin`; that alias is an implementation detail. Expose it only through advanced compatibility surfaces when existing callers need it, and label it as local-machine configuration.

Keep established neighboring contracts intact unless they are part of the same user-facing block. Source project repositories already use `projects[].repo.url`, so changing results repository config to `results.repo.remote` does not require renaming source project `repo.url`.

## Why This Matters

Portable config is commonly checked into a repository, copied into Docker development environments, or generated by setup scripts. A field that accepts a machine-local alias forces every machine and container to create the same Git remote name before the config works.

Using `remote` for the endpoint URL in the results repository block matches the user's model of "where this syncs", aligns with adjacent tools, and keeps Docker/latest-clone local development from depending on mutable host Git state.

## When to Apply

- A config file describes Git sync, artifact publishing, or result storage that should work from a fresh clone or container.
- A field could mean either a remote endpoint URL or a local Git remote-name alias.
- A setup script is expected to generate config that a developer can run with `bun run ...` from a latest checkout.

## Examples

**Prefer portable endpoint configuration:**

```yaml
results:
  repo:
    remote: https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv-private.git
    path: .agentv/remotes/agentv-private
    branch: agentv/results/v1
```

**Avoid making a checked-in config depend on a local alias:**

```yaml
results:
  repo:
    remote: origin
    path: .agentv/remotes/agentv-private
    branch: agentv/results/v1
```

If a local alias override must exist for backward compatibility, keep it on an explicitly advanced or legacy option, not on the ordinary project config contract.

## Related

- `packages/core/src/evaluation/loaders/config-loader.ts` parses nested results repository config and normalizes the endpoint URL into internal runtime fields.
- `packages/core/src/projects.ts` serializes project config and keeps `results.repo.remote` as the portable Git endpoint.
- `apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx` documents the dashboard remote-results setup path.

# Example AgentV home local config.
# Copy this file to .agentv/config.local.yaml (gitignored) and customise it for your machine.
# The entrypoint appends that override to the generated ~/.agentv/config.yaml after projects:.
#
# Mirrors are an optimisation for large workspace repos. If a mirror path is absent, AgentV
# falls back to cloning from GitHub using the configured credentials.
#
# scripts/run-local-dashboard-in-docker.sh bind-mounts any CargoWise / CargoWise.Shared /
# CargoWise.Customs / Glow clone found under CARGOWISE_HOST_DIR
# (default: ~/projects/WiseTechGlobal) read-only into /data/cargowise-host/<name>.
# Point the mirror entries below at those container paths to reuse your local clones.
git_cache:
  mirrors:
    https://github.com/WiseTechGlobal/CargoWise.git: /data/cargowise-host/CargoWise
    https://github.com/WiseTechGlobal/CargoWise.Shared.git: /data/cargowise-host/CargoWise.Shared

apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:24:The `dashboard` command launches a web-based dashboard for browsing evaluation runs, inspecting individual test results, and reviewing scores. It shows both local runs and runs synced from a remote results repository.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:42:Dashboard does not accept a run workspace directory or `index.jsonl` manifest as a direct source. It reads one configured run source per project: the project's `.agentv/results/` tree, plus an external results repository or run directory configured under `results:` in YAML. The old `.agentv/results/runs/**` layout is not a Dashboard-visible layout. For one-off inspection of a copied run bundle, use `agentv results report <run-workspace-or-index.jsonl>`.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:65:| `--multi` | Launch in multi-project dashboard mode (deprecated; use auto-detect or `--single`) |
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:168:Remote run payloads stay immutable, but their tags are editable. Dashboard writes remote tag changes as metadata overlays under `metadata/runs/.../tags.json` in the configured results repo clone/branch. That overlay path is a remote-results implementation detail, not part of the local `.agentv/results/<experiment>/<timestamp>/` layout. Remote tag overlays use the same `tag_revision` stale-write check as local tags. Until those overlays are synced, the run and project show a dirty state; **Sync Project** commits and pushes them when it is safe to do so.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:214:To register a remote repo and keep it synced automatically, add a nested `repo` block to the entry in `$AGENTV_HOME/config.yaml`. `repo.url` is the Git remote URL AgentV passes to `git clone`, so it can be HTTPS or SSH. `repo.branch` is the branch or ref to check out, and `repo.path` is the local checkout path:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:255:For local Docker development, prefer building from the latest AgentV checkout and running `bun` inside that image instead of depending on a globally installed or npm-installed `agentv` binary. A setup script should mount a writable `AGENTV_HOME`, project checkouts, and any results repo paths, publish the dashboard port on `0.0.0.0` when Tailscale access is needed, then start the dashboard with the local build, for example `bun apps/cli/dist/cli.js dashboard`. Keep this Docker path separate from Kubernetes or Helm deployment assets unless those assets are actually needed to model the local runtime.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:277:For a registered project, put results repo settings on that project's entry in `$AGENTV_HOME/config.yaml`:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:297:`results.repo.remote` is the Git remote URL used when AgentV creates a fresh results checkout, and the intended remote URL for portable project config. `results.repo.path: .` stores completed run artifacts on a dedicated branch of the source repository without checking out that branch in the source worktree. AgentV does not add or rewrite remotes inside an existing checkout; the checkout's existing `origin` must already point at the repository you want to fetch and push. When `results.repo.remote` is omitted, `results.repo.path` means an existing local Git checkout whose object database and refs AgentV should write to, and the branch defaults to `agentv/results/v1`. AgentV creates the branch automatically on first publish and commits only AgentV result paths into it. `sync.auto_push: false` keeps the result commit local; set it to `true` to push the branch best-effort after each completed run. For CI workflows where a push failure should fail the command after local artifacts are written, invoke the run with `agentv eval run --results-require-push`. `sync.push_conflict_policy` defaults to `block`; the removed `backup_and_force_push` value is rejected with migration guidance because AgentV never force-pushes result branches. Non-fast-forward result branch pushes are auto-merged with artifact-aware Git merge drivers and pushed as a fast-forward, so the canonical results branch is never force-pushed or rewritten. Genuine overlay conflicts route to a timestamped temp branch plus a GitHub compare link for a human merge instead.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:299:For a separate results repository, use `results.repo.remote` and an optional managed clone `results.repo.path`:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:317:`results.repo.remote` is the Git remote URL used for clone and push operations, so use HTTPS when credentials are HTTP-token based and SSH when the runtime has SSH keys configured. When `results.repo.remote` is set and `results.repo.path` is missing or empty, AgentV creates that filesystem location with `git clone`. If `results.repo.path` already points at a Git checkout, AgentV treats that checkout's remotes as user-owned state: it fetches and pushes using the existing configured remote name (`origin` by default), but it does not run `git remote add` or `git remote set-url`. Omit `results.repo.remote` only when `results.repo.path` points at an already-existing local checkout such as `.`.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:332:Project-local `.agentv/config.yaml` is for portable eval defaults such as `execution`, `eval_patterns`, and `dashboard`. Do not put `projects` in project-local config; AgentV warns and ignores it there. `results_by_project` is deprecated; use `projects[].results` in `$AGENTV_HOME/config.yaml`.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:337:- `projects[].results.repo.remote` is the git-backed results store remote URL. **Sync Project** fetches, fast-forwards, and, when configured, pushes run artifacts and mutable metadata in the local checkout at `projects[].results.repo.path`.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:377:Current flat fields (`path`, `repo_url`, `ref`, `results.repo_url`, `results.repo_path`, `results.branch`, `results.remote`, and `results.path`) still load with migration warnings and are written back in nested form the next time AgentV saves the project registry. Older removed fields (`source`, `repository`, `results.mode`, `results.repo` as a string, `results.repository`, `results.local_path`, and `results.auto_push`) fail validation with migration guidance.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:389:Adding a `results` block does **not** backfill those historical runs into the results branch automatically. Result publishing only affects runs created after the results repo is configured. `sync.auto_push` controls network push and best-effort WIP checkpoints for in-progress `agentv eval` runs.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:399:Once configured, Dashboard reads local runs and the configured results repo clone for that project. The status endpoint does not fetch from the remote on every page load; use **Sync Project** when you want to exchange changes with the results repo remote.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:420:**Sync Project** fetches the results repo and only changes the clone when Git says it is safe:
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:423:- Safe uncommitted changes under the configured results repo's owned result and metadata paths, such as remote tag overlays under `metadata/runs/**`, are committed and pushed when `sync.auto_push: true`.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:424:- A local results repo that is ahead is pushed when `sync.auto_push: true` and the committed paths are all under `.agentv/results/**`.
apps/web/src/content/docs/docs/tools/dashboard.mdx:429:When sync is blocked, Dashboard keeps the local clone intact and shows the `block_reason`, `dirty_paths` or `conflicted_paths`, `git_status`, and a compact `git_diff_summary` so you can resolve the results repo manually before syncing again.

}

is_registered_path() {
    local candidate="$1"
    printf '%s\n' "${REGISTERED_PATHS}" | grep -Fx -- "${candidate}" >/dev/null 2>&1
}

register_project() {
    local dir="$1"
    local name="${2:-$(basename "${dir}")}"
    local repo="${3:-}"
    local ref="${4:-main}"
    local id
    local ts

    [ -d "${dir}/.agentv" ] || return 0

    if is_registered_path "${dir}"; then
        return
    fi

    id=$(printf '%s' "${name}" | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' | tr -cs 'a-z0-9' '-' | sed 's/-*$//')
    ts=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    {
        printf '  - id: %s\n    name: %s\n    path: %s\n' "${id}" "${name}" "${dir}"
        if [ -n "${repo}" ]; then
            printf '    repo_url: https://github.com/%s/%s.git\n    ref: %s\n' \
                "${GITHUB_REPO_OWNER}" "${repo}" "${ref}"
            # Git-native results: publish completed runs to a dedicated branch
            # of the eval repo itself (never checked out in the worktree).
            printf '    results:\n      repo_path: .\n      branch: %s\n      remote: %s\n      sync:\n        auto_push: %s\n        require_push: %s\n        push_conflict_policy: %s\n' \
                "${AGENTV_RESULTS_BRANCH}" \
                "${AGENTV_RESULTS_REMOTE}" \
                "${AGENTV_RESULTS_AUTO_PUSH}" \
                "${AGENTV_RESULTS_REQUIRE_PUSH}" \
                "${AGENTV_RESULTS_PUSH_CONFLICT_POLICY}"
        fi
        printf '    added_at: "%s"\n    last_opened_at: "%s"\n' "${ts}" "${ts}"
    } >> "${PROJECTS_FILE}"
    REGISTERED_PATHS="${REGISTERED_PATHS}
${dir}"
}

scan_projects() {
    local dir="$1"
    local depth="$2"
    local child
    local name

    [ -d "${dir}" ] || return

    if [ -d "${dir}/.agentv" ]; then
        register_project "${dir}"
        return
    fi

```

The container discovers AgentV projects by recursively scanning `AGENTV_DISCOVER_ROOTS`
for directories containing `.agentv/`, then registers them in `~/.agentv/config.yaml`.
Staging and production default this to `/data/repos` with `AGENTV_DISCOVER_MAX_DEPTH=3`.

For the two deployment-managed repos, the entrypoint now also writes the upstream-native
`repo_url` fields and git-native run-artifact results config into `config.yaml`:

```yaml
projects:
  - id: wtg-ai-prompts
    name: WTG.AI.Prompts
    path: /data/repos/WTG.AI.Prompts
    repo_url: https://github.com/WiseTechGlobal/WTG.AI.Prompts.git
    ref: main
    results:
      repo_path: .
      branch: agentv/results/v1
      remote: origin
      sync:
        auto_push: true
        require_push: false
```

`WiseTechAcademy.Evals` uses the same results method with `repo_path: .`, `branch: agentv/results/v1`, and `remote: origin`.

The `repo_url` fields match the current `EntityProcess/agentv` remote project sync model:
- first sync: `git clone --depth 1`
- later syncs: `git pull --ff-only`

The container still performs an explicit sync during bootstrap so it can run deployment-specific
prep immediately afterwards, such as `bun install` and repo-local `.env` generation.

When a cloned benchmark repo contains a `package.json`, the entrypoint also runs `bun install`
before starting Studio so benchmark hooks can import repo-local dependencies.
         AGENT_TARGET=openai LLM_TARGET=openai GRADER_TARGET=openai
  export OPENAI_API_KEY=$(cat /var/run/agentv-secrets/openai-api-key)
  cd /data/repos/WTG.AI.Prompts
  bun /app/apps/cli/dist/cli.js eval run \
    evals/cargowise/database/data-transformation-pr50857-e2e.eval.yaml \
    --target pi-sdk-openai --no-results-push --output /tmp/pr50857-smoke
  ```
  Expected: `RESULT: PASS (2/2 scored >= 80%, mean ~90%)`.

## Results Sync

- Both projects (`WTG.AI.Prompts`, `WiseTechAcademy.Evals`) register with results sync enabled on
  branch `agentv/results/v1` (`AGENTV_RESULTS_BRANCH`), remote `origin`, `auto_push: true`.
- The sync conflict policy is `push_conflict_policy: backup_and_force_push`
  (`AGENTV_RESULTS_PUSH_CONFLICT_POLICY`). This backs up a diverged remote results ref before
  replacing it, so concurrent dashboard runs do not deadlock on a non-fast-forward push. The
  AgentV default is `block`.

## Local Docker Notes

- `bash scripts/run-local-dashboard-in-docker.sh` is the local-only Docker dashboard helper. It builds from `AGENTV_SOURCE_DIR`, a local latest AgentV clone, and serves Studio on `http://0.0.0.0:3117` by default so it is reachable over Tailscale when the host firewall permits it.

## Remote Results

Dashboard can display runs pushed to a remote git repository by other machines or CI alongside your local runs. Each run in the list carries a source badge: **local** (green) or **remote** (amber). For in-progress eval durability before final publish, AgentV writes [WIP checkpoints](/docs/tools/wip-checkpoints/) to `agentv/wip/...` branches; Dashboard lists them only after they are recovered locally or published to the normal results branch.

### Configuration

For a registered project, put results repo settings on that project's entry in `$AGENTV_HOME/config.yaml`:

```yaml
projects:
  - id: agentv
    name: AgentV
    repo:
      url: https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv.git
      branch: main
      path: /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv
    results:
      repo:
        remote: https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv.git
        path: .
        branch: agentv/results/v1
      sync:
        auto_push: false
        push_conflict_policy: block
```

`results.repo.remote` is the Git remote URL used when AgentV creates a fresh results checkout, and the intended remote URL for portable project config. `results.repo.path: .` stores completed run artifacts on a dedicated branch of the source repository without checking out that branch in the source worktree. AgentV does not add or rewrite remotes inside an existing checkout; the checkout's existing `origin` must already point at the repository you want to fetch and push. When `results.repo.remote` is omitted, `results.repo.path` means an existing local Git checkout whose object database and refs AgentV should write to, and the branch defaults to `agentv/results/v1`. AgentV creates the branch automatically on first publish and commits only AgentV result paths into it. `sync.auto_push: false` keeps the result commit local; set it to `true` to push the branch best-effort after each completed run. For CI workflows where a push failure should fail the command after local artifacts are written, invoke the run with `agentv eval run --results-require-push`. `sync.push_conflict_policy` defaults to `block`; the removed `backup_and_force_push` value is rejected with migration guidance because AgentV never force-pushes result branches. Non-fast-forward result branch pushes are auto-merged with artifact-aware Git merge drivers and pushed as a fast-forward, so the canonical results branch is never force-pushed or rewritten. Genuine overlay conflicts route to a timestamped temp branch plus a GitHub compare link for a human merge instead.

For a separate results repository, use `results.repo.remote` and an optional managed clone `results.repo.path`:

```yaml
projects:
  - id: agentv
    name: AgentV
    repo:
      path: /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv
    results:
      repo:
        remote: git@github.com:EntityProcess/agentv-examples-eval-results.git
        branch: agentv/results/v1
        path: /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv-examples-eval-results
      sync:
        auto_push: true
        push_conflict_policy: block
```

`results.repo.remote` is the Git remote URL used for clone and push operations, so use HTTPS when credentials are HTTP-token based and SSH when the runtime has SSH keys configured. When `results.repo.remote` is set and `results.repo.path` is missing or empty, AgentV creates that filesystem location with `git clone`. If `results.repo.path` already points at a Git checkout, AgentV treats that checkout's remotes as user-owned state: it fetches and pushes using the existing configured remote name (`origin` by default), but it does not run `git remote add` or `git remote set-url`. Omit `results.repo.remote` only when `results.repo.path` points at an already-existing local checkout such as `.`.

You can also set a top-level global fallback in the same file. This is used when the current project is not registered or its registry entry has no `results` block:

```yaml
results:
  repo:
    remote: https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv.git
    path: .
    branch: agentv/results/v1
  sync:
    auto_push: false
    push_conflict_policy: block
```
```yaml
projects:
  - id: agentv
    name: AgentV
    repo:
      url: https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv.git
      branch: main
      path: /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv
    results:
      repo:
        remote: https://github.com/EntityProcess/agentv-eval-results.git
        branch: agentv/results/v1
        path: /home/entity/projects/EntityProcess/agentv-eval-results
      sync:
        auto_push: true
```

Current flat fields (`path`, `repo_url`, `ref`, `results.repo_url`, `results.repo_path`, `results.branch`, `results.remote`, and `results.path`) still load with migration warnings and are written back in nested form the next time AgentV saves the project registry. Older removed fields (`source`, `repository`, `results.mode`, `results.repo` as a string, `results.repository`, `results.local_path`, and `results.auto_push`) fail validation with migration guidance.

Use project-level **Sync Project** as the results exchange workflow. It handles pulled remote runs, locally edited metadata, dirty state, and blocked conflict feedback in one project-scoped action.

There is no separate `agentv results remote status` or `agentv results remote sync` command. The `agentv results` CLI stays focused on local run workspaces; manual remote exchange is Dashboard/API-only, with eval auto-export covering the common CI/publisher path.

payload.message:
No. That block is legacy and not correct for latest AgentV project config.

Current preferred schema is nested:

```yaml
projects:
  - id: wtg-ai-prompts
    name: WTG.AI.Prompts
    repo:
      url: https://github.com/WiseTechGlobal/WTG.AI.Prompts.git
      branch: main
      path: /data/repos/WTG.AI.Prompts
    results:
      repo:
        remote: https://github.com/WiseTechGlobal/WTG.AI.Prompts.git
        path: .
        branch: agentv/results/v1
      sync:
        auto_push: true
        push_conflict_policy: block
```

If you intentionally rely on the checkout’s existing `origin`, this is also valid and simpler:

```yaml
results:
  repo:
    path: .
    branch: agentv/results/v1
  sync:
    auto_push: true
    push_conflict_policy: block
```

What is wrong with the snippet:

```yaml
results:
  repo_path: .
  branch: agentv/results/v1
  remote: origin
  sync:
    auto_push: true
    require_push: false
```

- `results.repo_path`, `results.branch`, and `results.remote` are legacy flat fields. They may still load in some paths with warnings, but latest docs prefer `results.repo.path` / `results.repo.branch`.
- `results.remote: origin` is a legacy local Git remote-name override. Latest portable config uses `results.repo.remote` for a Git URL, not a local alias.
- `results.sync.require_push` was removed from persistent config. Use per-run `agentv eval run --results-require-push` when CI should fail if publishing cannot push.
- `push_conflict_policy: backup_and_force_push` in `agentv-deploy` docs is also stale/removed. Latest allowed value is `block`; AgentV should not force-push result branches.

I found `agentv-deploy` still generating the old shape in `docker-entrypoint.sh` and documenting it in `README.md` / `AGENTS.md`. It should be updated before relying on latest AgentV validation.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.message: do you think this shape is confusin? maybe both project repo and results repo should use same repo shape?

Yes, it is confusing.

The current shape is conceptually close but the names diverge:

```yaml
repo:
  url: ...
  branch: ...
  path: ...

results:
  repo:
    remote: ...
    branch: ...
    path: ...
```

That makes users ask whether `repo.url` and `results.repo.remote` are different kinds of things. They are not, really: both identify a Git endpoint. The difference is historical/user-intent naming, not a strong domain distinction.

A cleaner long-term shape would be consistent:

```yaml
repo:
  url: https://github.com/WiseTechGlobal/WTG.AI.Prompts.git
  branch: main
  path: /data/repos/WTG.AI.Prompts

results:
  repo:
    url: https://github.com/WiseTechGlobal/WTG.AI.Prompts.git
    branch: agentv/results/v1
    path: .
  sync:
    auto_push: true
    push_conflict_policy: block
```

For same-repo results, this reads naturally too:

```yaml
results:
  repo:
    path: .
    branch: agentv/results/v1
```

My recommendation:
- Keep accepting `results.repo.remote` for compatibility.
- Add/standardize `results.repo.url` as the preferred spelling to align with project `repo.url`.
- Update docs/examples to use `url`.
- Warn only later, if ever, that `remote` is accepted but not preferred.
- Keep `results.remote` flat alias deprecated/legacy because that one really is confusing: it means local Git remote name like `origin`.

This is a small schema cleanup with high clarity payoff.

content.text: shouldn't url be remote

payload.message: shouldn't url be remote

If we were designing only the results repo block, `remote` is defensible. But across the whole AgentV config, I think `url` is clearer.

Reason: in Git, “remote” commonly means the local alias (`origin`), not the endpoint. That ambiguity is exactly what caused this stale config:

```yaml
results:
  remote: origin
```

For portable config, the field means “Git endpoint URL,” so `url` is less ambiguous and matches project repo config:

```yaml
repo:
  url: https://github.com/org/project.git

results:
  repo:
    url: https://github.com/org/project.git
```

I’d use this rule:

- `repo.url`: portable Git endpoint URL.
- `repo.path`: local checkout path.
- `repo.branch`: branch/ref.
- Avoid `remote` in persisted config unless it explicitly means a local Git remote alias, and then call it something like `remote_name`.

So yes, `remote` sounds natural, but because Git already uses it for aliases, I’d standardize on `url` for both project and results repos.

# Example YAML Config for Dolt Repo Sync

Here's an example YAML configuration for Dolt repository sync in `.beads/config.yaml`:

```yaml
# Dolt sync configuration
sync:
  remote: git+ssh://git@github.com/org/repo.git  # Primary Dolt remote URL

# Optional Dolt federation (for multi-peer sync)
federation:
  remote: dolthub://myorg/beads  # DoltHub or cloud remote
  sovereignty: T2  # Data sovereignty tier: T1, T2, T3, or T4

# Dolt history & sync behavior
dolt:
  auto-commit: on    # Create a Dolt commit after each successful write
  auto-push: false   # Opt-in for single-writer setups
  auto-push-interval: 5m   # Minimum time between auto-pushes
  auto-push-timeout: 30s   # Timeout for a single auto-push attempt
``` [1](#0-0) [2](#0-1) [3](#0-2) 

## Key Configuration Options

### Remote Configuration
- **`sync.remote`**: Primary Dolt remote URL (supports `dolthub://`, `gs://`, `s3://`, `az://`, `file://`, or git protocols like `git+ssh://`) [4](#0-3) 
- **`federation.remote`**: Dolt remote URL for federation setups [5](#0-4) 
- **`federation.sovereignty`**: Data sovereignty tier (`T1`-`T4`) controlling data residency restrictions [6](#0-5) 

### Sync Behavior
- **`dolt.auto-commit`**: Automatically create Dolt commits after write commands (default: `on`) [7](#0-6) 
- **`dolt.auto-push`**: Automatically push to Dolt remote after writes (opt-in, default: `false`) [8](#0-7) 
- **`dolt.auto-push-interval`**: Minimum time between auto-pushes (default: `5m`) [9](#0-8) 
- **`dolt.auto-push-timeout`**: Timeout for single auto-push attempt (default: `30s`) [10](#0-9) 

## Notes

The `sync.remote` key is a YAML-only configuration key that must be set in `config.yaml` rather than the database, as it's read during startup before the database is opened [11](#0-10) . For existing projects, you can also set the remote using `bd dolt remote add origin <url>` which will automatically write to `sync.remote` in the config file [12](#0-11) .

Wiki pages you might want to explore:
- [Workspace Setup and Configuration (gastownhall/beads)](/wiki/gastownhall/beads#4)
- [Troubleshooting and Recovery (gastownhall/beads)](/wiki/gastownhall/beads#8)

### Citations

**File:** docs/CONFIG.md (L125-127)
```markdown
  auto-push-interval: 5m  # Minimum time between auto-pushes
  auto-push-timeout: 30s  # Bound one push attempt when the remote is unreachable
```
```

**File:** docs/CONFIG.md (L170-175)
```markdown
- `federation.remote`: Dolt remote URL (e.g., `dolthub://org/beads`, `gs://bucket/beads`, `s3://bucket/beads`, `az://account.blob.core.windows.net/container/beads`)
- `federation.sovereignty`: Data sovereignty tier:
  - `T1`: Full sovereignty - data never leaves controlled infrastructure
  - `T2`: Regional sovereignty - data stays within region/jurisdiction
  - `T3`: Provider sovereignty - data with trusted cloud provider
  - `T4`: No restrictions - data can be anywhere
```

**File:** docs/CONFIG.md (L177-185)
```markdown
#### Example Configuration

```yaml
# .beads/config.yaml
# Optional: Dolt federation
federation:
  remote: dolthub://myorg/beads
  sovereignty: T2
```
```

**File:** website/docs/reference/configuration.md (L95-97)
```markdown
| `dolt.auto-commit` | `--dolt-auto-commit` | `BD_DOLT_AUTO_COMMIT` | `on` | Create a Dolt history commit after each successful write |
| `dolt.auto-push` | — | `BD_DOLT_AUTO_PUSH` | `false` | Auto-push to Dolt remote after writes (opt-in) |
| `dolt.shared-server` | `--shared-server` | `BEADS_DOLT_SHARED_SERVER` | `false` | Share one Dolt server at `~/.beads/shared-server/` |
```

Commit and push that config file with your normal git workflow. Other clones can

when they already have the right database.

For agent-orhcestrator this is the research:

# Example Workspace/Repo Configuration

Here are example YAML configurations for Agent Orchestrator, ranging from minimal to comprehensive setups.

## Minimal Configuration

The absolute minimum required configuration:

```yaml
projects:
  my-app:
    repo: owner/my-app
    path: ~/my-app
    defaultBranch: main
``` [1](#0-0) 

## Basic Configuration with Defaults

A typical configuration with sensible defaults:

```yaml
# agent-orchestrator.yaml
$schema: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ComposioHQ/agent-orchestrator/main/schema/config.schema.json
port: 3000

defaults:
  runtime: tmux       # default on macOS / Linux; on Windows the default is `process` (ConPTY)
  agent: claude-code
  workspace: worktree
  notifiers: [desktop]

projects:
  my-app:
    repo: owner/my-app
    path: ~/my-app
    defaultBranch: main
    sessionPrefix: app

reactions:
  ci-failed:
    auto: true
    action: send-to-agent
    retries: 2
  changes-requested:
    auto: true
    action: send-to-agent
    escalateAfter: 30m
  approved-and-green:
    auto: false # flip to true for auto-merge
    action: notify
``` [2](#0-1) 

## Full Configuration Example

The comprehensive example file with all available options:

```yaml
# Agent Orchestrator Configuration
# Copy to agent-orchestrator.yaml and customize.
$schema: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ComposioHQ/agent-orchestrator/main/schema/config.schema.json

# Web dashboard port
port: 3000

# Terminal server ports
terminalPort: 14800
directTerminalPort: 14801

# Power management
power:
  preventIdleSleep: true  # Default on macOS, no-op on Linux

# Lifecycle controls
lifecycle:
  autoCleanupOnMerge: true
  mergeCleanupIdleGraceMs: 300000

# Default plugins
defaults:
  agent: claude-code
  workspace: worktree
  notifiers: [desktop]

projects:
  my-app:
    name: My App
    repo: org/my-app
    path: ~/my-app
    defaultBranch: main
    sessionPrefix: app

    # Per-project plugin overrides
    agent: claude-code
    runtime: tmux

    # Agent-specific config
    agentConfig:
      permissions: skip
      model: opus

    # Files to symlink into workspaces
    symlinks: [.env, .claude]

    # Commands to run after workspace creation
    postCreate:
      - "pnpm install"

    # Inline rules
    agentRules: |
      Always run tests before pushing.
      Use conventional commits (feat:, fix:, chore:).
``` [3](#0-2) 

## Multi-Project Configuration

Example for managing multiple repositories:

```yaml
projects:
  frontend:
    repo: org/frontend
    path: ~/frontend
    sessionPrefix: fe

  backend:
    repo: org/backend
    path: ~/backend
    sessionPrefix: api

  docs:
    repo: org/docs
    path: ~/docs
    sessionPrefix: doc
``` [4](#0-3) 

## Local Project Config (Flat Format)

Modern project-local configuration without the `projects:` wrapper:

```yaml
agent: claude-code
runtime: tmux
workspace: worktree

symlinks:
  - .env
  - .claude

postCreate:
  - pnpm install

agentRules: |
  Run tests before pushing.
  Keep PRs small and focused.
``` [5](#0-4) 

## Notes

- The `$schema` line enables editor autocomplete and validation [6](#0-5) 
- Runtime defaults to `tmux` on macOS/Linux and `process` on Windows [7](#0-6) 
- Additional examples are available in the `examples/` directory [8](#0-7) 
- Configuration can be validated using `ao doctor` [9](#0-8) 

Wiki pages you might want to explore:
- [Agent Orchestrator Overview (AgentWrapper/agent-orchestrator)](/wiki/AgentWrapper/agent-orchestrator#1)
- [Getting Started: Installation & Quickstart (AgentWrapper/agent-orchestrator)](/wiki/AgentWrapper/agent-orchestrator#1.1)
- [Configuration Reference (AgentWrapper/agent-orchestrator)](/wiki/AgentWrapper/agent-orchestrator#6)

### Citations

**File:** SETUP.md (L186-192)
```markdown
```yaml
projects:
  my-app:
    repo: owner/my-app
    path: ~/my-app
    defaultBranch: main
```
```

**File:** SETUP.md (L624-640)
```markdown

projects:
  frontend:
    repo: org/frontend
    path: ~/frontend
    sessionPrefix: fe

  backend:
    repo: org/backend
    path: ~/backend
    sessionPrefix: api

  docs:
    repo: org/docs
    path: ~/docs
    sessionPrefix: doc
```
```

for entireio/cli this is the research:

Based on the codebase context, you're asking for configuration examples that set up repository sync for checkpoint data. The Entire CLI uses JSON configuration files to sync checkpoint branches to a separate repository via the `checkpoint_remote` setting. [1](#0-0) 

## JSON Configuration Example

Here's a complete `settings.json` example that configures checkpoint sync to a separate repository:

```json
{
  "enabled": true,
  "strategy_options": {
    "checkpoint_remote": {
      "provider": "github",
      "repo": "myorg/checkpoints-private"
    }
  }
}
```

This configuration should be placed in `.entire/settings.json` in your repository root. [2](#0-1) 

## Configuration Details

The `checkpoint_remote` object requires two fields: [3](#0-2) 

- **`provider`**: The git hosting provider (e.g., "github")
- **`repo`**: The repository in "owner/repo" format (must contain a slash) [4](#0-3) 

## Alternative: CLI Configuration

You can also configure this via the CLI instead of editing the JSON file directly: [5](#0-4) 

```bash
entire enable --checkpoint-remote github:myorg/checkpoints-private
```

## How It Works

When configured, Entire will: [6](#0-5) 

1. Derive the checkpoint URL using the same protocol (SSH or HTTPS) as your push remote
2. Push the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch to the specified checkpoint repository instead of your default push remote
3. Skip pushing if a fork is detected (when push remote owner differs from checkpoint repo owner) [7](#0-6) 
4. Fetch the checkpoint branch locally if it exists on the remote but not locally (one-time setup) [8](#0-7) 

## Notes

- The configuration only supports JSON format, not YAML, based on the codebase implementation
- The `repo` field must contain a slash (e.g., "org/repo") or the configuration will be rejected [4](#0-3) 
- Legacy string format (e.g., `"checkpoint_remote": "git@github.com:org/repo.git"`) is no longer supported and will be ignored [9](#0-8)

# Example YAML Config for Dolt Repo Sync

Here's an example YAML configuration for Dolt repository sync in `.beads/config.yaml`:

```yaml
# Dolt sync configuration
sync:
  remote: git+ssh://git@github.com/org/repo.git  # Primary Dolt remote URL

# Optional Dolt federation (for multi-peer sync)
federation:
  remote: dolthub://myorg/beads  # DoltHub or cloud remote
  sovereignty: T2  # Data sovereignty tier: T1, T2, T3, or T4

# Dolt history & sync behavior
dolt:
  auto-commit: on    # Create a Dolt commit after each successful write
  auto-push: false   # Opt-in for single-writer setups
  auto-push-interval: 5m   # Minimum time between auto-pushes
  auto-push-timeout: 30s   # Timeout for a single auto-push attempt
``` [1](#0-0) [2](#0-1) [3](#0-2) 

## Key Configuration Options

### Remote Configuration
- **`sync.remote`**: Primary Dolt remote URL (supports `dolthub://`, `gs://`, `s3://`, `az://`, `file://`, or git protocols like `git+ssh://`) [4](#0-3) 
- **`federation.remote`**: Dolt remote URL for federation setups [5](#0-4) 
- **`federation.sovereignty`**: Data sovereignty tier (`T1`-`T4`) controlling data residency restrictions [6](#0-5) 

### Sync Behavior
- **`dolt.auto-commit`**: Automatically create Dolt commits after write commands (default: `on`) [7](#0-6) 
- **`dolt.auto-push`**: Automatically push to Dolt remote after writes (opt-in, default: `false`) [8](#0-7) 
- **`dolt.auto-push-interval`**: Minimum time between auto-pushes (default: `5m`) [9](#0-8) 
- **`dolt.auto-push-timeout`**: Timeout for single auto-push attempt (default: `30s`) [10](#0-9) 

## Notes

The `sync.remote` key is a YAML-only configuration key that must be set in `config.yaml` rather than the database, as it's read during startup before the database is opened [11](#0-10) . For existing projects, you can also set the remote using `bd dolt remote add origin <url>` which will automatically write to `sync.remote` in the config file [12](#0-11) .

- [Workspace Setup and Configuration (gastownhall/beads)](/wiki/gastownhall/beads#4)
- [Troubleshooting and Recovery (gastownhall/beads)](/wiki/gastownhall/beads#8)

- `federation.remote`: Dolt remote URL (e.g., `dolthub://org/beads`, `gs://bucket/beads`, `s3://bucket/beads`, `az://account.blob.core.windows.net/container/beads`)
- `federation.sovereignty`: Data sovereignty tier:
  - `T1`: Full sovereignty - data never leaves controlled infrastructure
  - `T2`: Regional sovereignty - data stays within region/jurisdiction
  - `T3`: Provider sovereignty - data with trusted cloud provider
  - `T4`: No restrictions - data can be anywhere
